reasoning: All five candidates pass the six gates and all hygiene checks; the round is decided on behavioral probing the gate cannot see. The two discriminators are (1) five DISTINCT growth stages under --ascii and (2) single-sourcing the win/loss screen text per tip 7. Only C and E keep all five stages distinct in ASCII (A collapses to 1 glyph, B to 2, D to 4 with near-win==win). Of those two, C carries a genuine tip-7 violation: it defines MSG_WIN_TITLE/MSG_LOSS_TITLE in game.py but leaves them DEAD while ui.py re-types the same header literals (two copies that can drift) — the most-repeated seed defect across prior rounds. E is the only candidate that keeps all five ASCII stages distinct AND imports the screen header/body constants into ui.py and tests (tip-7 clean), with dedicated SPEC 6.6/6.7 end screens, a guarded is_lost directly tested on the won-at-zero-water boundary (tip 10), try/finally-pinned win/loss e2e tests, and an explicit assertion that --ascii output contains zero non-ASCII chars. Its only blemishes are non-defects: an inert smell (the win/loss title held in status_message contains an emoji, but the end-screen renderer ignores that field so no ASCII leak ever occurs — verified empirically) and a literal-wording nit (the growth glyph derives win/loss from predicates rather than the state flags, which agree on every reachable state). No behavioral defect was found in E. C is a close second and would also be defensible; B is the most readable but its ASCII stages collapse to 2; A lacks dedicated end screens and drops the loss water-sense text.
progression: E delivers the complete game in one shot — engine, UI, CLI, and a 88-test suite — from the base commit (no prior merged Word Garden code existed; this is round 1 of WG5). It builds directly on the seeded cross-project tips and visibly absorbs them: injectable input_fn/output_fn/rng with a module-level (non-seeded) random default (tip 6); argparse with no SystemExit swallowing so --help exits 0 and usage errors exit 2 (tip 4); select_word/water_for that raise ValueError on unknown difficulty and empty pool, with tests forcing the path (tip 5); a standalone is_lost carrying its own not-won guard and a dedicated test on the won-at-zero-water boundary (tip 10); message constants single-sourced and imported into ui.py and tests, including the win/loss screen headers and bodies (tip 7); forced-scenario win/loss e2e tests with a pinned word restored in try/finally (tips 3, 8); and faithful SPEC 6.6/6.7 end screens whose terminal text is asserted, not just the flags (tip 1). What is newly proven this round and feeds the next briefing as tips 11-15: the --ascii fallback must keep the five growth stages mutually DISTINCT (not just emoji-free) — a collapse the gate cannot catch and which sank A/B/D; dedicated end screens must carry both the SPEC header and the out-of-water text; state fields must stay render-neutral (no embedded glyphs); the win/loss render branch should read the state flags rather than recompute predicates; and win/loss screen text is message text governed by tip 7. E is the cleanest embodiment of these, so adopting it advances the merged state to a fully playable, spec-faithful game while the new tips harden the field against the ASCII-collapse and screen-text-duplication defects that this round exposed.
